questions.prompt: Complete: "English ___ in many countries around the world."
questions.explanation: The subject "English" receives the action, so we use the present simple passive: be + past participle = is spoken. "speaks"/"speak" are active; "is speaking" is the active present continuous, which makes English the doer rather than the thing spoken.

questions.prompt: Choose the passive form of: "They built this bridge in 1990."
questions.explanation: The past simple passive is was/were + past participle: this bridge was built in 1990. (b) uses the present "is" with a finished past time; (c) has no auxiliary, so it is not passive; (d) uses the base form "build" instead of the past participle "built".

questions.prompt: Complete: "The letters ___ yesterday by the postman."
questions.explanation: The plural subject "the letters" and the past time "yesterday" require were + past participle: were delivered. "was delivered" uses the singular "was" with a plural subject; "are delivered" is present, not past; "delivered" alone (active) makes the letters the doer.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct sentence. The agent is unknown.

options.text: My bike was stolen last night.
My bike was stolen by someone last night.
Someone was stolen my bike last night.

questions.explanation: When the doer is unknown or unimportant, we use the passive and simply leave out the "by" phrase: my bike was stolen last night. (b) adds an empty "by someone", exactly the kind of useless agent the passive lets us drop; (c) is muddled (the bike, not someone, was stolen); (d) is active and uses the wrong form.

questions.prompt: Complete: "This song ___ by millions of people every day."
questions.explanation: "listen" needs the preposition "to", which stays in the passive: this song is listened to. "listens"/"is listening" are active; "is listened" drops the required "to", leaving the verb incomplete.

questions.prompt: Choose the passive form of: "Someone cleans the offices every morning."

options.text: The offices are cleaned every morning.
The offices is cleaned every morning.
The offices were cleaned every morning.
The offices are cleaning every morning.

questions.explanation: The present simple passive with a plural subject is are + past participle: the offices are cleaned. "is cleaned" uses the singular auxiliary with a plural subject; "were cleaned" is past, but "every morning" is present; "are cleaning" is the active continuous, making the offices the doer.
